findings.description: The PDF has a centered header block with title, venue, address, and date centered on the page, while the HTML renders the title as a large left-aligned heading with a horizontal rule, and the subtitle and venue info are left-aligned.
findings.evidence: PDF: 'New and Watauga Hydrologic Model / Stakeholder Meeting #2' is centered and bold, with venue info centered below. HTML: Title is large left-aligned heading, 'Stakeholder Meeting #2' is a left-aligned subheading, venue info is left-aligned.

findings.description: The title formatting differs: PDF has both lines as bold centered text of similar size, while HTML renders the main title much larger than the subtitle with a decorative horizontal rule separator.
findings.evidence: PDF: 'New and Watauga Hydrologic Model' and 'Stakeholder Meeting #2' are similar-sized bold centered text. HTML: Main title is significantly larger with an underline/rule, subtitle is smaller.

findings.description: The agenda items in the PDF use a two-column layout with time slots as a distinct left column and descriptions/presenters on the right, while the HTML renders time and description inline on the same line without clear columnar separation.
findings.evidence: PDF: Time slots (e.g., '9:00 - 9:10') are in a visually separate left column with agenda items indented to the right. HTML: Time and description flow together on the same line.

findings.description: The document title and subtitle are split into h1 and h2, which is reasonable, but 'Stakeholder Meeting #2' is presented as a separate h2 heading when in the PDF it is part of the centered title block along with the main title. This is an acceptable structural interpretation, not a true hierarchy issue.
findings.evidence: PDF: 'New and Watauga Hydrologic Model / Stakeholder Meeting #2' as a single centered title block. HTML: h1 'New and Watauga Hydrologic Model' and h2 'Stakeholder Meeting #2'.
